active_ingredients: Ibuprofen 200 mg per tablet/caplet (OTC); 400 mg, 600 mg, 800 mg tablets by prescription
indications_and_usage: Uses: temporarily relieves minor aches and pains due to headache, toothache, backache, muscular aches, the common cold, minor pain of arthritis, and menstrual cramps. Temporarily reduces fever. It is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), so it also reduces inflammation/swelling (e.g., a sprained ankle, sore joints).
contraindications: Do not use if you have ever had an allergic reaction to ibuprofen, aspirin, or any other NSAID (symptoms such as hives, facial swelling, asthma/wheezing, shock). Do not use right before or after heart bypass surgery.
boxed_warning: NSAIDs can cause an increased risk of serious cardiovascular thrombotic events (heart attack, stroke) and serious gastrointestinal bleeding, ulceration, and perforation of the stomach or intestines, which can be fatal; risk rises with dose and duration of use.
warnings_and_cautions: Stomach bleeding warning: this product contains an NSAID, which may cause severe stomach bleeding; risk is higher in people age 60 or older, with a history of ulcers or bleeding, who take a blood thinner or steroid, who take more or longer than directed, or who have 3 or more alcoholic drinks a day. May also cause kidney injury and raise blood pressure. Ask a doctor before use if you have heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, asthma, or are in the last 3 months of pregnancy (NSAIDs are not recommended after about 20 weeks of pregnancy). Take with food or milk if it upsets your stomach.
adverse_reactions: Common: heartburn, nausea, stomach pain, dyspepsia, diarrhea or constipation, dizziness, headache. Serious (uncommon): GI ulcer/bleeding, kidney impairment, fluid retention/edema, raised blood pressure, allergic reactions.
drug_interactions: Acetaminophen: ibuprofen (an NSAID) and acetaminophen act by different mechanisms and can generally be taken together or alternated for better pain/fever control, as long as each drug's own maximum daily dose is not exceeded; some combination ibuprofen + acetaminophen products exist. Don't double up on acetaminophen hidden in cold/flu products.
Warfarin and other anticoagulants/antiplatelets: increased risk of serious GI and other bleeding when combined with ibuprofen.

Low-dose aspirin (for heart/stroke protection): ibuprofen can interfere with aspirin's antiplatelet effect if taken close together; if both are needed, take aspirin at least 30 minutes before or 8 hours after ibuprofen, and discuss with a clinician.
Lithium: NSAIDs including ibuprofen can raise serum lithium levels and cause lithium toxicity; avoid the combination or have levels monitored by the prescriber.

ACE inhibitors (e.g., lisinopril), ARBs, and diuretics: ibuprofen can blunt their blood-pressure-lowering effect and, especially in older adults or dehydration, increase the risk of acute kidney injury (the 'triple whammy').

Other NSAIDs (naproxen, aspirin, ketorolac, meloxicam, diclofenac) and corticosteroids (such as prednisone): do not combine ibuprofen with another NSAID - it adds GI ulcer, kidney, and cardiovascular risk without extra pain relief; combining ibuprofen with oral steroids markedly increases GI bleeding risk.

SSRIs and SNRIs - sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine, citalopram, venlafaxine, duloxetine: ibuprofen and other NSAIDs combined with these antidepressants raise the risk of GI bleeding and bruising; use the lowest effective dose, with food, and consider gastroprotection with a PPI if regular use is needed.
Methotrexate: ibuprofen can raise methotrexate blood levels - avoid the combination at high methotrexate doses and use caution with weekly low-dose methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis.
dosage_and_administration: Adults and children 12 years and over (OTC): take 200 mg every 4 to 6 hours; if pain or fever does not respond, 400 mg may be used. Do not exceed 1200 mg in 24 hours unless directed by a doctor. Use the smallest effective dose for the shortest time. Take with food or milk if it upsets your stomach.
By prescription, under medical supervision, the maximum is 3200 mg per day in divided doses (e.g., for rheumatoid arthritis or osteoarthritis).

Children under 12 years: use a children's ibuprofen product and dose by weight (about 5-10 mg/kg per dose every 6-8 hours) as directed by a doctor; do not use in infants under 6 months without a doctor's advice.
overdosage: Symptoms of overdose can include nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, drowsiness, dizziness, ringing in the ears, and, in large overdoses, low blood pressure, kidney failure, GI bleeding, or seizures. Contact a Poison Control Center (1-800-222-1222) or seek emergency care.
